context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Participatory Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What are the ways in which participation can be achieved? What's the measure of participation and how must one judge it? Can the tension between participation as a goal and as a method be resolved and if so how? What were the structural factors that made participation such a key element in development strategies?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection on participatory development, and I’m pretty unsure how to organize my response to the prompt. I think I get the general idea of participation, but I’m confused about how to address the “goal vs method” tension clearly.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point. Let's dig into the confusion first. In your own words, what do you think it means for participation to be a *goal* versus a *method*? Just take a shot at it — don't worry about being precise yet.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the start of the assignment and asking for organizational help. My role is not to structure the paper for them, but to help them think through the concepts first. I'll use the Socratic method to probe what they already understand, starting with the goal vs. method tension since that's what they flagged as confusing. This will help them discover the structure naturally rather than being handed one.

exchanges.student: I think participation as a method means using local people to help make development projects work better, and participation as a goal means people are involved... maybe because that’s considered more democratic in itself? I’m not totally sure if the difference is basically efficiency versus empowerment, but that’s my guess.
exchanges.tutor: That's a promising distinction. Now push it a bit further: if participation is used mainly as a *method* for efficiency, what might get sacrificed or ignored about the people involved? And conversely, if it's treated purely as a *goal*, what practical challenges might arise for actually getting development done?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a solid intuitive distinction - efficiency vs. empowerment is actually quite close to the core tension. I should affirm the direction without confirming it as complete, and push them to think deeper about what the tension *between* these two actually looks like in practice. This will help them move toward the 'can the tension be resolved' part of the prompt.

exchanges.student:
I think if it’s mainly a method, then people can get treated like tools or sources of information instead of real decision-makers. Like officials might say a project is participatory just because they held meetings, even if local people had no actual power.

If it’s treated only as a goal, then maybe decision-making gets slower or messier, and it can be hard to agree on priorities. I’m a little stuck on how to say that without sounding like participation is a bad thing.
exchanges.tutor: You're not saying participation is bad — you're identifying a real trade-off, which is good critical thinking. Don't get stuck on the framing; the tension you described is valid. Now, let's shift: *why* do you think participation became such a central idea in development planning in the first place? What was happening historically or structurally that made it feel necessary?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has developed the tension quite well and is now worried about tone. This is a minor concern that shouldn't hold them back - I should briefly address it but push them toward the bigger picture. They've done enough thinking on this tension to start connecting it to the other parts of the prompt, particularly the structural factors that made participation central to development strategies.

exchanges.student: I think it became central because top-down development strategies were criticized for failing or ignoring local needs, and maybe also because states and big institutions lost some legitimacy. I’m guessing structural adjustment and the influence of donors/NGOs also mattered, but I’m fuzzy on how to connect those clearly to participation.
exchanges.tutor: Good instincts. Here's a connecting question: if top-down state-led development was seen as failing *and* states were losing legitimacy, why would international donors and NGOs see participation as the solution? What does participation offer them, practically and politically?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is identifying relevant structural factors - top-down failures, legitimacy crises, structural adjustment, donor/NGO influence. These are all valid threads. Rather than helping them connect the dots, I should ask a focused Socratic question to help them draw the connection themselves, particularly around why donors/NGOs would push participation as a response to those failures.

exchanges.student:
Maybe participation gave donors and NGOs a way to make projects seem more legitimate and locally grounded, especially when states weren’t trusted. Practically, it could also give them local knowledge and cooperation, so projects might work better or face less resistance.

I’m not sure if that sounds too cynical though.
exchanges.tutor: That's not cynicism — that's a legitimate analytical observation scholars make. Hold onto it. You've now touched on most parts of the prompt, but you haven't addressed one yet: *how do you measure or judge participation?* What would make you say a process was genuinely participatory versus just performative?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a sophisticated point about the instrumental use of participation by external actors, which is academically legitimate and not merely cynical. I should validate the analytical direction briefly and push them to connect this back to the prompt's question about 'measures of participation' - they've covered methods, goals, tension, and structural factors reasonably well, but haven't yet tackled how participation is measured or judged.

exchanges.student:
I’d probably judge it by whether people actually had influence over decisions, not just whether they were present at meetings. Maybe things like who sets the agenda, who speaks, whether marginalized groups are included, and whether participation changes outcomes.

I feel like I need a cleaner way to phrase that in the paper.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong set of criteria. Note that I won't help with phrasing — that part is yours to work out. But you now have ideas covering all four parts of the prompt. I'd encourage you to try writing a first draft and see how it comes together. What feels like the hardest part to actually put on paper?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid grasp of the measure of participation - influence over decisions, agenda-setting, inclusivity, and outcomes. Their instinct to 'phrase it more cleanly' is a writing concern, but per the rules I should not help with language or phrasing. I should redirect them to writing, since we've now covered all four parts of the prompt across several exchanges and it's time to integrate. This is also a good moment to push them toward a first draft attempt.

exchanges.student: Honestly, the hardest part is turning these points into a clear structure instead of just listing answers to each question. I think my draft might get choppy, so I could use help figuring out a simple argument shape.
exchanges.tutor: Structuring is your job, not mine — but here's a useful question to get you there: across everything you've said so far, what is the *one central claim* you want a reader to walk away with? If you can articulate that, structure tends to follow.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to structure their paper for them, which falls under tasks I should not complete. Per the rules, I should not organize or suggest a structure. I should redirect them to think about what their own central argument or thesis is, which will naturally generate structure. The student has enough material to do this themselves.

exchanges.student:
I think my main claim is that participation matters in development, but it’s not automatically meaningful, and it has to be judged by power and actual influence, not just inclusion on paper. Maybe also that it became important because of the failures and legitimacy problems of top-down development.

Is that too broad for one reflection?
exchanges.tutor: Check it against the prompt yourself: does your claim address *all four* questions asked — ways to achieve participation, how to measure it, whether the goal/method tension can be resolved, and the structural factors behind its rise? What do you notice?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a reasonable central claim and is now second-guessing its scope. Rather than evaluating it for them, I should push them to assess it themselves against the prompt's specific questions, which will help them see whether their claim covers the necessary ground without me making that judgment for them.

exchanges.student: I think it covers measurement and the structural factors pretty well, and kind of covers the goal/method tension, but it doesn’t clearly include the ways participation can be achieved. So I probably need to add something about institutional design or decision-making processes, not just the idea of participation in general.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. You've identified the gap yourself — that's the important skill. You now have everything you need to start writing. Go ahead and produce a draft, and come back if you hit a specific conceptual wall.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done a strong self-assessment and correctly identified the gap. They've now covered all the conceptual ground needed and have a clear sense of what to add. This is a good moment to push them to start writing rather than continuing to plan, since we're close to the planned conversation length and they have sufficient material.

exchanges.student: Okay, that helps. I’m going to try a draft built around the idea that participation only counts when it shifts power, and then I’ll include a section on how it can be achieved through actual decision-making structures, not just consultation.
exchanges.tutor: That sounds like a solid direction. Go write it — come back when you have a draft and want to think through any specific part of it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a focused, substantive thesis and a clear sense of structure on their own through guided discovery. This is a good outcome. I should briefly affirm the direction and encourage them to write, without adding more scaffolding or content that could substitute for their own work.
